coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots prepared with polyethyleneimine as both reducing agent and stabilizer for synthesis of Ag/CQDs composite for Hg2+ ions detection
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ting
Dong, Jiang Xue
Liu, Shi Gang
Li, Na
Lin, Shu Min
Fan, Yu Zhu
Lei, Jing Lie
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description: A stable silver nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots (Ag/CQDs) composite was prepared by using CQDs as reducing and stabilizing agent. The CQDs synthesized with polyethyleneimine (PEI) showed an extraordinary reducibility. When Hg2+ was presented in the Ag/CQDs composite solution, a color change from yellow to colorless was observed, accompanied by a shift of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band and decrease in absorbance of the Ag/CQDs composite. On the basis of the further studies on TEM, XPS and XRD analysis, the possible mechanism is attributed to the formation of a silver-mercury amalgam. Hence, a two dimensional sensing platform for Hg2+ detection was constructed upon the Ag/CQDs composite. Based on the change of absorbance, a good linear relationship was obtained from 0.5 to 50μM for Hg2+. And the limit of detection for Hg2+ was as low as 85nM, representing high sensitivity to Hg2+. More importantly, the proposed method also exhibits a good selectivity toward Hg2+ over other metal ions. Besides, this strategy demonstrates practicability for the detection of Hg2+ in real water samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dots
Polyethyleneimine
Surface plasmon resonance
Hg2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CQDs and Ag/CQDs composite preparation and Hg2+ detection
Characterization of CQDs and Ag/CQDs composite
Mechanism for CQDs and Ag/CQDs composite formation
Detection mechanism of Ag/CQDs composite for Hg2+
Exploration for the detection conditions of Hg2+
Sensitivity and selectivity for Hg2+ detection
Determination of Hg2+ in tap water and lake water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the mechanism for the synthesis of CQDs, and the comparison of Ag/PEI and Ag/CQDs.
Absorption spectra of PEI, CQDs and the synthesized Ag/CQDs composite. Inset: photographs of PEI (left), CQDs (middle) and the Ag/CQDs composite (right) under natural light.

(A) TEM image and (B) XPS full scan spectrum of CQDs. (C) TEM image and (D) high-resolution Ag 3d XPS spectrum of Ag/CQDs composite. (E) FTIR spectra of PEI (a), CQDs (b) and Ag/CQDs composite (c). (F) XRD patterns of the CQDs (a) and the Ag/CQDs composite (b). Insets of (A) and (C) are HRTEM images of CQDs and Ag/CQDs composite, respectively.
(A) TEM, (B) HRTEM images and (C) XPS full scan spectrum of Ag/CQDs composite after addition of Hg2+. (D) XPS spectrum of Hg 4f.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of Ag/CQDs composite upon different concentrations of Hg2+ (from top to bottom: 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 8, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 and 80μM). (B) The relative absorption intensity ΔA over the concentration of Hg2+ is within the range of 0–100μM, and the linear range for Hg2+ is 0.5–50μM. (In the inset picture: the concentrations of Hg2+ are 0–80μM). (C) The relative SPR band shift Δλ dependent on the concentration of Hg2+ is from 0 to 100μM, and the linear range for Hg2+ is 8–70μM. UV–vis absorption spectra (D), ΔA (E) and Δλ (F) of the proposed sensing system in the presence of various metal ions. The concentrations of all metal ions are 50μM in (D) and the inset photographs of (E).
Analytical results of Hg2+ in tap water and lake water samples (n=3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ND defined as “Not Detection”, aAbsorbance change, b SPR band shift.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots prepared with polyethyleneimine as both reducing agent and stabilizer for synthesis of Ag/CQDs composite for Hg2+ ions detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, No.2 Tiansheng Road, BeiBei District, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A stable silver nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots (Ag/CQDs) composite was prepared by using CQDs as reducing and stabilizing agent. The CQDs synthesized with polyethyleneimine (PEI) showed an extraordinary reducibility. When Hg2+ was presented in the Ag/CQDs composite solution, a color change from yellow to colorless was observed, accompanied by a shift of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band and decrease in absorbance of the Ag/CQDs composite. On the basis of the further studies on TEM, XPS and XRD analysis, the possible mechanism is attributed to the formation of a silver-mercury amalgam. Hence, a two dimensional sensing platform for Hg2+ detection was constructed upon the Ag/CQDs composite. Based on the change of absorbance, a good linear relationship was obtained from 0.5 to 50μM for Hg2+. And the limit of detection for Hg2+ was as low as 85nM, representing high sensitivity to Hg2+. More importantly, the proposed method also exhibits a good selectivity toward Hg2+ over other metal ions. Besides, this strategy demonstrates practicability for the detection of Hg2+ in real water samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Monitoring of mercury ion (Hg2+) in aquatic ecosystems is always an important issue because it is one of the most toxic heavy metal pollutants and exists broadly in water. It can cause serious damages to kidney, brain, endocrine system and central nervous system of both humans and animals [1–3]. Conventional analytical techniques for Hg2+ detection include atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [4], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICPMS) [5], and X-ray fluorescence spectrometry (XRF) [6]. These methods are both selective and sensitive, but require sophisticated instrumentation and time-consuming sample preparation [7]. To address this problem, the development of cost-effective, convenient, and applicable methods for Hg2+ detection is highly desired. Colorimetric methods, using metal nanoparticles as optical sensors [8,9], are extremely attractive because the detection results can be clearly read out by using a simple spectrophotometer or even by naked eyes [1,10].

In the past few years, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have been extensively exploited as a colorimetric probe based on the change in the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption of AgNPs solution [10–12]. The SPR property is influenced by many parameters, such as capping agent, the refractive index of medium, and the size, shape and state of AgNPs [13,14]. Therefore, finding a way to synthesize suitable AgNPs is significant for application in colorimetric probe. A variety of conventional reducing and stabilizing agents, for instance, trisodium citrate, sodium borohydride and polyvinylpyrrolidone have been used for the synthesis of AgNPs [15–17].

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as an advanced support material for the synthesis of AgNPs by a simple and fast way [18,19]. Compared to the traditional reducers [20,21], the AgNPs synthesized by CQDs have a more uniform size, better stability, and superior dispersion, because the CQDs have high specific surface area and the reduction process can be easily controlled. Thus, there opens a new application of CQDs. Considering that they are excellent electron donors and electron acceptors [22,23], CQDs have promising potential to be an reducing or oxidizing agent [24]. Based on the reducing property of CQDs, Yang et al. introduced sodium borohydride into the CQDs solution to prepare AgNPs [25]. Liu et al. synthesized AgNPs by using CQDs as a reducing agent in boiled water (100°C) with NH3·H2O [19].

In this work, for the development of a class of water-soluble CQDs with reducing property, polyethyleneimine (PEI) was used as the carbon source through one-step hydrothermal method to yield the reductive CQDs. As a water-soluble cationic polymer containing primary, secondary, and tertiary amino groups [26], PEI has been applied to synthesis of AgNPs with other reducing agents [27,28], UV irradiation [29] or high temperature [30]. However, the CQDs prepared with PEI can be used for synthesis of AgNPs without the need of other reducing agents, which exhibit stronger reducing efficiency than PEI. The synthesized Ag/CQDs composite solution can be stored under ambient conditions for five months, and no precipitant has been found. The existence of Hg2+ will decrease the absorbance of Ag/CQDs composite, accompanied by the color change, for silver-mercury (AgHg) amalgam has been formed in Ag/CQDs composite solution. Therefore, a convenient Ag/CQDs composite-based dual-signal (absorbance change and SPR band shift) probe for monitoring the concentration of Hg2+ was provided.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI, M w =10 000, 99%), silver nitrate (AgNO3), mercuric nitrate (Hg(NO3)2), and 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd., Shanghai, China. The metal salts used, including Al(NO3)3·9H2O, Ba(NO3)2, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, KNO3, LiNO3, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, NaNO3, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, CH3COONa, NaCl, Na2CO3, NaF, Na3PO4·12H2O, and Na2SO4 were of analytical reagent grade and were used as received. All the solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm).

The fluorescence measurements were recorded on a Hitachi F-2700 spectrofluorophotometer (Japan). The slit widths were set at 10.0nm for both excitation and emission, and the PMT voltage was set at 400V. A UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used for measuring the optical absorption spectra. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were observed from a JEM-2100 system operated at 200kV (Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed with an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Electron, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were conducted using a D8 DISCOVER X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The XPS and XRD samples were analyzed by coating colloidal CQDs, solid Ag/CQDs composite, and solid AgHg amalgam on highly cleaned silicon wafers. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of colloidal PEI and CQDs, and solid Ag/CQDs composite were taken with a Bruker IFS 113v spectrometer (Germany) after pelleted with KBr. For comparison purposes, the concentration of Hg2+ in water samples was determined by cold vapour atomic absorption spectrometry (CVAAS) using DMA–80 (Milestone, Italy).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs and Ag/CQDs composite preparation and Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs prepared through hydrothermal method is described as follows: 1mL of PEI (1%, w/w) was placed in a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (3mL) and heated at 200°C for 4h. After the reactor cooled down to room temperature naturally, the resultant homogeneous solution without further processing was the CQDs that we used in the assay. The Ag/CQDs composite was synthesized by a chemical reduction of AgNO3 with CQDs as the reducing agent. Firstly, 100μL of CQDs solution was added to the freshly prepared AgNO3 solution (800μL, 10mM) under vigorous stirring for 2min, then the mixture solution was reacted at 90°C for 50min. Finally, a purplish red Ag/CQDs composite solution was obtained. For the detection of Hg2+, 3μL of Ag/CQDs composite and 5μL of Hg2+ within a range of 0–10mM were added to 490μL of HEPES buffer (pH 6.0, 20mM) successively, and the pH value was unchanged. Then, the absorption spectra were conducted in the range of 200–600nm after the mixture was incubated at room temperature about 30s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and Ag/CQDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectrum collected from the CQDs solution (Fig. 1 ) displays a broad absorption band centered at 360nm, while PEI alone has no absorption above 260nm. The absorption peak at 360nm in CQDs can be attributed to the n-π* transition from −CONH− bond [31]. The n-π* transition is from the CO bond and results in an absorption band, but −NH− bond can promote the red shift of this band, then a band at 360nm was produced. After the addition of Ag+, a new peak appeared at 394nm, which corresponded to the characteristic absorption of the AgNPs induced by surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption [32]. However, the absorption peak at 360nm from CQDs disappeared, indicating that the amount of CQDs did not exceed.

The morphology of CQDs was explored by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which reveals that CQDs are well-dispersed and have a small diameter around 1−2nm as shown in Fig. 2 A. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows that this kind of CQDs without any obvious lattices (inset of Fig. 2A). The TEM image of Ag/CQDs composite in Fig. 2C shows that the size (∼5nm) is larger than that of CQDs, which may be because the CQDs have been attached to the surface of AgNPs [18,33]. The interplanar d-spacing of Ag/CQDs composite calculated from HRTEM image (Fig. 2C, inset) was found to be 0.235nm, which was consistent with the (111) plane of the typical AgNPs [12,13]. The results indicate that the AgNPs were prepared successfully.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) full scan spectrum (Fig. 2B) shows that CQDs contain C, N, and O elements, and the contents of the three elements were calculated to be 67.85%, 30.15%, and 2.00%, respectively. The C1s scan spectrum (Fig. S1A) could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 284.5eV (CC) and 285.5eV (CN) [31,34], respectively. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. S1B) of CQDs can be fitted into two peaks at 398.3 and 399.3eV, which are assigned to N in a graphite-like structure and OCN group, respectively [35,36]. The C 1s and N 1s spectra demonstrate that amide groups are formed within CQDs. The two fitted peaks at 531.0 and 532.6eV from the O 1s spectrum (Fig. S1C) are ascribed to CO and COH/COC groups, respectively [7,37]. Compared to the XPS spectrum of CQDs, the XPS full scan spectrum of the Ag/CQDs composite (Fig. S1D) presents a new peak of Ag element. Moreover, the intensity of O element was increased whereas the intensity of N element was decreased. In the C 1s spectrum (Fig. S1E), a new peak at 288.0eV can be fitted in comparison with CQDs, which is attributed to the CO group [34]. Based on this feature, we inferred that CN bond of CQDs was disrupted during the formation of Ag/CQDs composite, while more CO group was formed. The Ag 3d spectrum (Fig. 2D) reveals two peaks at binding energy values of 367.6 and 373.7eV for 3d 5/2 and 3d 3/2, suggesting the metallic nature of silver [13].

Next, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 2E) was used to evaluate the characteristic peaks of CQDs and Ag/CQDs composite. PEI (curve a, Fig. 2E) has the typical absorption bands at 3352 and 3285, 2885, and 1126cm−1 corresponding to stretching vibrations of primary amino group, CH2, and CN bonds, respectively [38]. The absorption band at 1598cm−1 is associated with NH bending vibration of primary amino group. The FTIR spectrum of CQDs (curve b) represents a new peak at 1637cm−1 which can be assigned to the amide group (CONH), and the peak at 3362cm−1 is associated with the secondary amino group [39,40]. Three peaks at 1474 and 1314, and 1110cm−1 are respectively assigned to bending and stretching vibrations of OH [41]. Moreover, the peak at 2838cm−1 may be attributed to the vibration of C−H from aldehyde group. When the Ag/CQDs composite was synthesized (curve c), the intensities of the bands for secondary amino group at 3426cm−1 and CONH group at 1638cm−1 are decreased, indicating that the Ag/CQDs composite was formed. Additionally, the absorption band of OH group almost disappeared whereas a strong band at 1381cm−1 for CH bending appeared, which may belong to the oxidized reducing aldehyde group [13]. These results are corresponding to those obtained from the XPS spectra of Ag/CQDs composite. Besides, all the mentioned bands in Fig. 2E are displayed in Table S1.

To further explore the formation of Ag/CQDs composite, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 2F, CQDs exhibit a broad diffraction peak centered around 23° (curve a), which is ascribed to the highly disordered carbon atoms [18]. As for the Ag/CQDs composite (curve b), the characteristic peaks of crystalline AgNPs at 2θ=38°, 44°, 64°, and 78° respectively corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) lattice planes are observed [14,42]. Additionally, the peak at 23° of CQDs was extremely decreased after the formation of Ag/CQDs composite, which demonstrated that the disordered carbon atoms of CQDs were disappeared when CQDs were attached to the surface of AgNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for CQDs and Ag/CQDs composite formation
ce:section.ce:para: According to the analysis above, the proposed mechanism for CQDs and Ag/CQDs composite formation were explored. As shown in Scheme 1 , during the synthesis of CQDs, PEI chains were broken under the high temperature (200°C), and the chain scission occurs primarily at the tertiary and secondary amino groups, yielding the amide and aldehyde groups [29]. Broken chains were further carbonized into CQDs with these groups on their surface, which has been demonstrated by XPS and FTIR spectra. With these surface groups, the reducibility of CQDs was improved. Thus, CQDs have a stronger ability to reduce Ag+ than PEI, and can save more raw material. When the Ag/CQDs composite was generated by using CQDs as the reducing agent, the FTIR spectrum of Ag/CQDs composite (Fig. 2E, curve c) shows that the absorption band of CONH and OH groups was decreased compared to that of CQDs, and appeared a new band for −COO− group. The XPS spectrum (Fig. S1E) also suggested that more CO groups were formed in the Ag/CQDs composite than those in CQDs. These results prove that the amide and aldehyde groups of CQDs were broken to carboxyl group after the formation of Ag/CQDs composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of Ag/CQDs composite for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions for producing the Ag/CQDs composite have been optimized carefully (Supplementary material). After optimizing the synthesis conditions of Ag/CQDs composite, Hg2+ was added in the mixture. The TEM measurement was carried out to investigate the morphological change of Ag/CQDs composite. Fig. 3 A shows that the presence of Hg2+ results in much larger spherical nanoparticles. In the HRTEM image (Fig. 3B), the d-spacing 0.221, 0.243, and 0.251nm correspond to the (4 2 0), (3 3 0) and (4 0 0) planes of AgHg amalgam [43]. Thus, the mechanism of the size change can be ascribed to that the AgHg amalgam was formed as a coating on the Ag/CQDs composites [12]. Fig. 3C depicts the XPS spectrum of Ag/CQDs composite after addition of Hg2+, which shows that the intensity of Ag 3d is decreased and the peak of Hg 4f appears. It is attributed to the redox reaction between zero-valent silver and Hg2+, for the difference in the standard redox potentials of Hg2+/Hg (0.85V) and Ag+/Ag (0.8V) couples [44,45]. The Hg 4f spectrum of AgHg particles presents two peaks at 101.5 and 105.6eV (Fig. 3D) for 4f 7/2 and 4f 5/2, suggesting the presence of metallic Hg [43]. These observations strongly demonstrate that AgHg amalgam has been formed. Furthermore, the XRD analysis of Ag/CQDs composite after the addition of Hg2+ is performed in Fig. S5. As displayed in curve b (Fig. S5), the characteristic peaks are very similar to those of Ag/CQDs composite (curve a, Fig. S5), but the intensities of these peaks are much lower than those of Ag/CQDs composite. The mechanism for Hg2+ detection is that AgNPs reduced Hg2+ to Hg and disintegrated into smaller particles from Ag/CQDs composite at first. Then AgNPs and Hg formed AgHg amalgam on the surface of Ag/CQDs composite and caused a decrease in absorbance and SPR band shift of Ag/CQDs composite [21,46,47] as shown in Fig. S3. Hence, Hg2+ can be detected by two signals in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration for the detection conditions of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The assay conditions, such as pH value, reaction time and the volume of Ag/CQDs composite, were optimized for Hg2+ detection. The effect of different pH values on the detection of Hg2+ was investigated. As shown in Fig. S6A and S6B , the relative absorption intensity ΔA has the highest value at pH 6.0, and the relative SPR band shift, Δλ, has a small distinction in the pH range of 5.5−7.0. Thus pH 6.0 was chosen for the sensitive detection of Hg2+. At room temperature, when Hg2+ was introduced to the Ag/CQDs composite solution, ΔA and Δλ can reach to their steady state rapidly, and remain stable for a long time (Fig. S6C and S6D). Therefore, the reaction of this system does not require fine control of time. As depicted in Fig. S6E and S6F , the ratio of absorption intensity (A 0–A)/A 0 and Δλ were decreased with increasing volume of Ag/CQDs composite. Considering that low level of Ag/CQDs composite would narrow the linear range of Hg2+, the middle volume of 3μL was selected. Moreover, the effect of the size of Ag/CQDs composite for the detection of Hg2+ was investigated in Fig. S7, which indicates that the Ag/CQDs composite synthesized by longer time shows smaller size, and has greater sensitivity, like Meelapsom’s work [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimum conditions, the sensitivity of Ag/CQDs composite for Hg2+ was investigated. It can be seen in Fig. 4 A that the absorption peak of Ag/CQDs composite decreases gradually with increasing concentration of Hg2+, and the SPR band of Ag/CQDs composite displays a distinct blue shift at the same time, which is attributed to the formation of AgHg amalgam between Ag and Hg. Based on these phenomena, there is a favorable linear correlation (R =0.9978) between ΔA and Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.5 to 50μM (Fig. 4B), and the detection limit for Hg2+ relied on 3δ/slope was estimated to be 85nM. The color of the sensing system, following the addition of Hg2+, changed from pale yellow to colorless (Fig. 4B, inset), which provides a platform for a colorimetric method for Hg2+ detection. Meanwhile, Fig. 4C presents a good linear relationship (R =0.9908) between Δλ and Hg2+ concentration in the range of 8 to 70μM, and the limit of the detection (defined as 3δ/slope) was approximately 1μM. By comparison, 50μM Hg2+ was added to the CQDs solution to investigate whether Hg2+ has effect on the stability of the sole CQDs. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs just has a slight change, which proves that Hg2+ has hardly influence on the CQDs. Furthermore, compared to other colorimetric methods based on AgNPs (Table S2), the Ag/CQDs composite exhibits a lower detection limit for Hg2+ detection.

To evaluate the specificity of this dual-mode sensing system, fifteen other metal ions, including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+ were selected to investigate the selectivity of the strategy for the detection of Hg2+. The absorption spectra of Ag/CQDs composite solutions before and after addition of various metal ions are given in Fig. 4D. Upon addition of 50μM Hg2+, the absorption peak of Ag/CQDs composite decreased significantly with a blue shift. The absorption spectrum is almost unchanged when 50μM other metal ions (except for Fe3+) was added to the Ag/CQDs composite solution. However, Fe3+ increased the absorption of Ag/CQDs composite, which may be because the absorption band of Fe3+ has partly overlapped with the SPR band of Ag/CQDs composite (Fig. S9). As presented in Fig. 4E and F, even the concentration of other metal ions was added to 500μM, the change of both ΔA and Δλ is negligible. The color of the sensing system dose not change after the addition of other metal ions (50μM), except for Hg2+ which makes the system from yellow to colorless (Fig. 4E, inset). Some common anion ions (CH3COO−, Cl−, F−, CO3 2–, NO3 −, SO4 2–and PO4 3–) were also investigated as shown in Fig. S10, and they were unaffected to the detection of Hg2+. These results demonstrate that our strategy shows outstanding selectivity toward Hg2+ sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ in tap water and lake water
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of this method, we used tap water and lake water samples for the practical applications. The tap water was derived from our dormitory without any pretreatment. The lake water obtained from Chongde Lake in our university was boiled at first, then filtered through a 0.2μm microporous membrane to remove particulates and centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min finally. We have not found Hg2+ in real samples. The recoveries of Hg2+ in real samples were measured, which are in acceptable range of 94.45%−104.50%. Furthermore, the result of this purposed method has a good agreement with that obtained from CVAAS (Table 1 ). This indicates that our strategy has the potential for the determination of Hg2+ in drinking water and environmental water samples.

In this report, the stable Ag/CQDs composite was prepared using AgNO3 as the precursor and CQDs as both reducing agent and stabilizer. The CQDs synthesized by PEI presents a stronger reducing efficiency than PEI, because the amide and aldehyde groups were formed on the surface of CQDs at 200°C, and the obtained Ag/CQDs composite was more sensitive for the detection of Hg2+ than the Ag/PEI composite. With increasing amount of Hg2+, the color of Ag/CQDs composite was changed from yellow to colorless and the absorbance of Ag/CQDs composite was decreased, which was ascribed to the AgHg amalgam formation. Therefore, a sensitive and selective dual-signal sensing method based on the Ag/CQDs system was designed for the detection of Hg2+. Moreover, a simple and fast approach for monitoring the concentration of Hg2+ in environment water was developed, demonstrating great potential application in real analysis in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21273174) and the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (No.CSTC-2013jjB00002, CSTC-2015jcyjB50001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2016.10.034.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
